data.title: Judge allows Trump subpoena collecting info on Jewish students, faculty at Penn
data.description:
A federal judge ruled Tuesday the University of Pennsylvania has to comply with a subpoena from the Trump administration requesting lists of Jewish faculty, students and organizations and their contact information as part of a discrimination probe.  

Judge Gerald Pappert ruled the U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) is within its constitutional right to collect the information from Penn.

“For their legal arguments, respondents contend the charge of discrimination is invalid and the subpoena violates the United States Constitution in various ways. But the charge is valid and the constitutional claims are easily dispensed with,” Pappert, of Philadelphia’s Federal District Court, wrote in his ruling.  

The university has until May 1 to turn over the information, although it does not have to include an employee’s affiliation with specific Jewish organizations.  

Pappert wrote the subpoena was narrowly tailored to Jewish individuals on campus who could have seen or experienced antisemitism in the workplace. 

The EEOC said the agency has no comment on the ruling. 

The judge also dismissed concerns sharing this information with the government could put people in danger and balked at comparisons made to the Third Reich. 

“[C]omparing the EEOC’s investigation into antisemitism at Penn with Nazi Germany is counterproductive, as counsel acknowledged by attempting to backtrack from such analogies,” he wrote.  

The Hill has reached out to Penn for comment. 

The university has fought this subpoena since last summer even while making a deal with the Trump administration to restore federal funding that was cut over its previous policies regarding transgender athletes.  

The administration has gone after many universities over accusations of antisemitism, from Ivy Leagues such as Penn and Harvard to state schools including the University of California. 

This ruling could give the federal government more tools for antisemitism investigations into other schools, though critics accuse the administration of using antisemitism as an excuse to target colleges with which it disagrees.

data.title: David Byrne showing 'ICE footage' during 'Life During Wartime' performances
data.description:
Talking Heads frontman David Byrne on Monday said he has played “ICE footage” as he performs the song “Life During Wartime” while touring to promote his new album, “Who is the Sky?”

Byrne brought up the footage when asked by “The Late Show” host Stephen Colbert if Talking Heads songs like “Psycho Killer” and “Life During Wartime” feel “different in the context of today than when you first performed them.”

“My goodness. The second one, ‘Life During Wartime,’ yes,” Byrne said. “I licensed some ICE footage shot at the very end of the song. If we did [play the footage] for the whole song, it would be depressing.”

“But we showed in the very end and we started with some footage that I saw. It looked like a delivery guy on a bike being chased by the ICE guys and he gets away,” he said as the audience applauded. Byrne added that the clip debuted during his show in Chicago.

Comedian John Mulaney, who also appeared as a guest on Colbert’s show, said he was at the concert and witnessed the “biggest, coolest reaction I’ve ever seen from a live audience, ever.”

“The times we live in, we need some color,” Byrne later added.

The musician has been a vocal critic of the Trump administration’s actions. He slammed the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, calling it in an interview on “BBC Newsnight” earlier this month “completely illegal.”

“I’m certainly not a supporter of the Iran government, but we have no right to just start bombing them,” he told host Faisal Islam. “No one voted for it, no one decided it, the public did not endorse it, it’s just a small group of maniacs who decided they were going to do it, and I think Britain should be very careful following down that path.”

Byrne is one of many artists and other celebrities who have lambasted President Trump’s second administration, its policies and its actions. Many participated in the third iteration of “No Kings” demonstrations across the country over the weekend.

Artists and celebrities led demonstrations outside the Kennedy Center on Friday to denounce what they said were the administration’s threats to free speech. Actor Jane Fonda and musician Joan Baez, both longtime activists, were among the notable celebrities outside the arts center.

Fonda’s organization, the Committee for the First Amendment, led the event. She revived the group in October after ABC News temporarily suspended Jimmy Kimmel’s late-night show amid pressure from the government. It originally formed to support the Hollywood Ten, a group of left-wing screenwriters and directors targeted by the House Un-American Activities Committee in 1947.

data.title: FAA restricts some landings in San Francisco
data.description:
The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) is restricting the landing of some flights at San Francisco International Airport, the agency said Tuesday.

In a statement, the FAA said the airport “will experience some flight delays due to a runway repaving project and an FAA safety measure.” The agency added in the statement that its safety measure bars landing pathways for planes in which they are “side-by-side” and aiming for the airport’s “parallel east-west runways in clear weather when the pilots acknowledge having the other aircraft in sight”

“It requires staggered approaches, with one aircraft offset from the aircraft on the parallel runway. The FAA never allowed side-by-side approaches in bad weather,” the FAA said.

The FAA’s San Francisco landing measure follows recent turmoil at airports, with Transportation Security Administration (TSA) officers receiving retroactive paychecks on Monday for weeks in which they have worked without pay amid a shutdown of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

DHS’s funding challenge has caused many of its employees to go without pay, causing absences and departures among TSA employees. Due to the absences and departures, multiple terminal checkpoints at airports closed.

The terminal closures lengthened wait times, as a wait time for one checkpoint at George Bush Intercontinental Airport stretched between three and 90 minutes earlier this month, per Houston outlet KIAH.

data.title: Trump rails against White House ballroom challenge, vows to fix 'filthy' Reflecting Pool
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday railed against a lawsuit challenging the White House ballroom construction and other projects as a judge ruled construction on the ballroom must be halted.

A federal judge ordered the administration on Tuesday to halt the construction of the ballroom until it receives congressional approval. The suit was brought by the National Trust for Historic Preservation.

“So, the White House Ballroom, and The Trump Kennedy Center, which are under budget, ahead of schedule, and will be among the most magnificent Buildings of their kind anywhere in the World, gets sued by a group that was cut off by Government years ago, but all of the many DISASTERS in our Country are left alone to die,” Trump wrote on Truth Social. “Doesn’t make much sense, does it?”

U.S. District Judge Richard Leon ruled in favor of the trust’s request for a preliminary injunction but suspended enforcement of it for 14 days, saying the case “raises novel and weighty issues, that halting an ongoing construction project may raise logistical issues,” according to The Associated Press.

He said any work that has to do with the safety and security of the White House is excluded from the injunction.

The White House ballroom construction started last fall with the demolition of the East Wing. The administration has said it will be 90,000 square feet, and Trump has said it will fit nearly 1,000 guests.

The project is being paid for by private donors, according to Trump.

Leon’s decision stems largely from Trump bypassing Congress to do construction work on a federally owned complex.

“The President of the United States is the steward of the White House for future generations of First Families,” Leon wrote at the start of his ruling. “He is not, however, the owner!”

Trump did not get permission from Congress before starting the construction, and the plans did not go through the input phase from the National Capital Planning Commission and the Commission of Fine Arts, which is required.

Trump said shortly after the ruling became public that the trust should instead go after renovations underway at the Federal Reserve, which Trump has railed against as he continues to criticize Chairman Jerome Powell for not lowering interest rates more aggressively.

“The National Trust for Historic Preservation sues me for a Ballroom that is under budget, ahead of schedule, being built at no cost to the Taxpayer, and will be the finest Building of its kind anywhere in the World,” Trump wrote. “I then  get sued by them over the renovation of the dilapidated and structurally unsound former Kennedy Center, now, The Trump Kennedy Center (A show of Bipartisan Unity, a Republican and Democrat President!), where all I am doing is fixing, cleaning, running, and ‘sprucing up’ a terribly maintained, for many years, Building, but a Building of potentially great importance.”

“Yet, The National Trust for Historic Preservation, a Radical Left Group of Lunatics whose funding was stopped by Congress in 2005, is not suing the Federal Reserve for a Building which has been decimated and destroyed, inside and out, by an incompetent and possibly corrupt Fed Chairman,” he said.

In a separate post, he announced that he and Secretary of the Interior Doug Burgum “are working on fixing the absolutely filthy Reflecting Pool between the Lincoln Memorial and the Washington Monument.”

“This work was supposed to be done by the Biden Administration, but Sleepy Joe doesn’t know what ‘CLEAN’ or proper maintenance is — The President and Secretary do!” he wrote.

The reflecting pool is just the latest beautification project that Trump has taken on in Washington. Along with the ballroom, Kennedy Center, and reflecting pool, the president also has his sights set on local golf courses and a new triumphal arch.

data.title: Social media giants not fully complying with child account ban, Australia alleges
data.description:
Social media platforms hosted by Meta, TikTok, Snapchat and YouTube, could face court enforcement in Australia for allegedly not fully complying with the country’s ban on the platforms for those under 16, the country’s digital safety watchdog said in a new report published Thursday. 

The compliance report, the first of its kind since the platform ban took effect, detailed how Australia’s eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant has “significant concerns” about the compliance of Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat, TikTok and YouTube with the ban, which began last December

The ban, which was imposed on 10 platforms, is the world’s first social media ban for kids younger than 16. Aside from those being investigated, the other platforms subject to the ban include Kick, Reddit, Threads, X and Twitch. These platforms face fines up to 49.5 million Australian dollars ($32.99 million) if they do not take steps to remove the accounts of those under 16. 

A press release on the report said the commissioner saw “some progress” in the first three months, but multiple “poor practices” remain regarding compliance. These include prompting kids to attempt age assurance even when their declared age is under 16 and allowing children under 16 to repeatedly try the same age assurance method to get a 16+ result. 

Grant further found the platforms failed to provide “accessible or effective pathways” for age-restricted accounts, and did not have sufficient ways to prevent under 16 accounts from being created.

Notifications were sent to the platforms, along with a warning about potential enforcement action. 

“While social media platforms have taken some initial action, I am concerned through our compliance monitoring that some may not be doing enough to comply with Australian law,” Grant said in the release. “As a result, we are now moving into an enforcement stance.

The commissioner acknowledged change takes time, but argued the platforms are able to “comply today” and expect companies to do so. 

When reached for comment, a spokesperson for Meta said the company is “committed” to complying with Australia’s social media ban and “working constructively with eSafety and the government.” 

“We’ve also been clear that accurately determining age online is a challenge for the whole industry, particularly at the age‑16 boundary where the government’s own age assurance technology trial noted ‘natural error margins,’” the spokesperson added, adding the company will “keep investing in enforcement to detect and remove under-16 accounts and support parents, while advocating for a system that’s workable in practice and delivers better safety outcomes.” 

The issue of age verification is also a hot topic in the U.S., where the large technology companies also disagree on how to verify age. Meta argues the onus is on the app store and operating system to verify users’ ages, while app store hosts like Google say the onus is on both the stores and developers. 

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: Trump moves Forest Service HQ from DC to Utah
data.description:
The Trump administration will move the headquarters for the U.S. Forest Service from Washington, D.C., to Salt Lake City, it announced Tuesday.

In a press release, the Department of Agriculture said the move will also be accompanied by a “sweeping restructuring” at the agency.

A department spokesperson told The Hill that 260 positions will move out of the D.C. region and 130 will remain in Washington, while the number of additional relocations is “unknown.”

The headquarters move is expected to be complete by summer 2027.

The spokesperson did not directly address The Hill’s questions about whether the restructure would involve layoffs. 

“Moving the Forest Service closer to the forests we manage is an essential action that will improve our core mission of managing our forests while saving taxpayer dollars and boosting employee recruitment,” Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins said in a statement alongside the announcement.

The decision to move the headquarters echoes actions from the first Trump administration, which saw the Bureau of Land Management’s headquarters move to Colorado for similar reasons. 

At that time, critics warned that forcing D.C.-based staff to head West would result in people quitting their jobs and could result in a brain drain at the agency. 

As for its restructure, the Forest Service said it would establish 15 state directors to oversee its operations.

It also said that it would close all of its regional offices, shifting many functions to service centers in Albuquerque, N.M.; Athens, Ga.; Fort Collins, Colo.; Madison, Wis.; Missoula, Mont.; and Placerville, Calif. 

The Forest Service said it will “consolidate” leadership of its research activities. Instead of its multiple dispersed research stations with their own leadership structures, it will move its research to one location in Fort Collins.

The agency said its restructure will “also drive a review and consolidation of facilities nationwide.”

“Additional phases of the reorganization, including the formal elimination of regional and station office structures and the full transition to a state-based model, will be implemented over the coming year,” the press release said.

data.title: Hundreds of hospitals threatened by Medicaid cuts: Research
data.description:
More than 400 hospitals across the country are in danger of closing or reducing services because of cuts to Medicaid funding under the GOP’s massive reconciliation package last year, according to new research. 

A report from Public Citizen found that 446 hospitals — 267 in urban areas and 176 in rural areas — are at “heightened risk” of having to pare down their services or completely shutter. 

These hospitals served around 6.6 million patients in 2024 and employed 275,000 direct patient workers, according to this new research. 

“The cuts will be devastating to many low-income and disabled individuals who rely on Medicaid,” Public Citizen researcher Eileen O’Grady wrote in the report.

“Moreover, they will have knock-on effects on hospitals that disproportionately serve these communities, deepening the financial strain already plaguing rural and safety-net hospitals and compromising their ability to deliver care, potentially leading many to close,” she continued. 

The progressive nonprofit consumer advocacy organization analyzed hospital finance data from 2022 to 2024 to identify institutions most likely to be hit the hardest financially by this legislation.

President Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act into law on July 4, 2025. The controversial megabill, which faced unilateral opposition from Democrats, imposed new work requirements to qualify for Medicaid and allowed for the expiration of premium tax credits. 

The nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office estimated after the bill’s passage last June that its provisions would increase the number of uninsured people by 16 million in 2034. 

In response to some concerns from more moderate Republicans during the bill’s negotiation process, the Senate’s GOP leaders doubled the size of the rural hospital relief fund from $25 billion to $50 billion. Democrats criticized this move, arguing it disproportionately favored red states. 

Most of the at-risk hospitals identified in Public Citizen’s report are concentrated in blue states. California, the most populous U.S. state, had the greatest number of at-risk hospitals at 83. New York also ranked high with 45 hospitals, as did Illinois with 28 and Washington with 22.

data.title: Watch live: Trump signs executive order at White House
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday afternoon will sign an executive order from the White House.

The event is scheduled to begin at 5 p.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Army suspends aircrew flying helicopters in fly-by near Kid Rock's Nashville estate
data.description:
The U.S. Army has suspended the aircrew that flew of pair of AH‑64 Apache helicopters near singer Kid Rock’s Nashville, Tenn., estate over the weekend, the service’s spokesperson confirmed to The Hill on Tuesday. 

“The personnel involved have been suspended from flight duties while the Army reviews the circumstances surrounding the mission, including compliance with relevant FAA regulations, aviation safety protocol and approval requirements,” Maj. Montrell Russell, an Army spokesperson, said in a statement. 

As the Saturday incident is under investigation, “it would be inappropriate to discuss specific findings at this time,” Russell said, adding that the Army “takes any allegations of unauthorized or unsafe flight operations very seriously and is committed to enforcing standards and holding personnel accountable.”

Two Apache helicopters from the 101st Combat Aviation Brigade at Fort Campbell, Ky. flew in the Nashville area on Saturday, including near the home of Robert James Ritchie, aka Kid Rock, a residence he dubbed “The Southern White House.” 

Rock posted the video of the encounter on social media with the caption “This is a level of respect that s‑‑‑ for brains Governor of California will never know. God Bless America and all those who have made the ultimate sacrifice to defend her.”

In the video, Rock can be seen saluting one of the helicopters that hovered near the home, which had gone viral. On Monday, the Army confirmed that it opened a review into the incident. 

“This incident is now under an Army Regulation 15-6 administrative investigation,” Russell said on Tuesday.

data.title: Judicial panel dismisses challenge to Wisconsin congressional map
data.description:
A three-judge panel in Wisconsin dismissed a challenge to the state’s congressional map on Tuesday, ruling that it lacks the authority to rule on the matter. 

The judges, Julie Genovese of the Dane County Circuit Court, Emily Lonergan of the Outagamie County Circuit Court and Mark Sanders of the Milwaukee County Circuit Court, wrote in an 18-page ruling that the Wisconsin Supreme Court did not provide them with proper guidance in assigning them the case.

Given that fact, the judges concluded that they have “no authority to supersede decisions” of the state Supreme Court.

The lawsuit, filed by 11 Wisconsin residents last July, alleged that Wisconsin’s congressional map violated the separation-of-powers doctrine and resulted from “unlawful partisan gerrymandering.” The Wisconsin Supreme Court appointed the panel to the case in November.

The Badger State has six Republicans and two Democrats in the House, representatives elected under congressional maps drawn following the 2010 census. Wisconsin Gov. Tony Evers (D) vetoed new maps that the GOP-controlled legislature drew in response to the 2020 census.

But the panel noted that while it is “not endorsing” the current congressional map, it does have the authority to “read into a Wisconsin Supreme Court case an analysis that it does not contain,” referring to multiple lawsuits filed against the map that the high court previously ruled on. 

The dismissal of the case is the latest development in a monthslong redistricting battle between Republican- and Democratic-led states across the country, a fight that began when the GOP-controlled Texas legislature — at President Trump’s urging — redrew the Lone Star State’s maps last year.

Zach Bannon, the central regional press secretary for the National Republic Congressional Committee, called the Wisconsin panel’s ruling a “significant win for Republicans and a yet another blow to desperate Democrats” seeking to “reshape” the electoral landscape.

“By keeping Wisconsin’s current district lines in place for 2026, Republicans are in a strong position to build on our momentum to retain and grow our House majority,” Bannon added.
